subject: SCIENCE
title: Materials Can Change
objective: Observe safe examples of changes from heating or cooling.
introduction: Heating or cooling can cause physical changes; some are reversible and others are not. Use on-screen observations only. You will explore examples, sort cards, practice, and answer five questions.

vocabulary.definition: A key word used in the lesson “Materials Can Change”.
A key word used in the lesson “Materials Can Change”.
A key word used in the lesson “Materials Can Change”.

activities.instruction: Study the idea and listen to the narration.
activities.content: Heating or cooling can cause physical changes; some are reversible and others are not. Use on-screen observations only.
activities.accessibilityAlternative: Text alternative: Heating or cooling can cause physical changes; some are reversible and others are not. Use on-screen observations only.

activities.instruction: Explore each learning token, then read the examples.
activities.accessibilityAlternative: A numbered list of the same examples is available for keyboard and screen-reader use.

activities.instruction: Sort each card into “fits the lesson” or “does not fit.”

feedback.correct: Your groups follow the lesson rule.
feedback.retry: Use the explanation, not color or position.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: Tap an item and then tap a labeled category; dragging is optional.

activities.instruction: What happens to ice in a warm room?

feedback.retry: Read the lesson rule again. It becomes metal. and It disappears from matter. do not fit.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: All choices are visible text and selectable with TalkBack.

activities.instruction: Match each example to the lesson idea.

feedback.retry: Return to the explanation and compare one pair at a time.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: A list-based matching control replaces drag gestures.

activities.instruction: Complete the guided review in order.

feedback.correct: You are ready for the five-question check.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: Up and down buttons provide an alternative to dragging.

items.prompt: Which statement best matches “Materials Can Change”?

options.text: Heating or cooling can cause physical changes; some are reversible and others are not. Use on-screen observations only.

items.explanation: The correct response is “Heating or cooling can cause physical changes; some are reversible and others are not. Use on-screen observations only.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Heating or cooling can cause physical changes; some are reversible and others are not. Use on-screen observations only.
The correct response is “ice melts” because it follows the lesson explanation: Heating or cooling can cause physical changes; some are reversible and others are not. Use on-screen observations only.

items.prompt: Which choice does not follow the lesson idea?
items.explanation: The correct response is “Every material melts” because it follows the lesson explanation: Heating or cooling can cause physical changes; some are reversible and others are not. Use on-screen observations only.

items.prompt: What happens to ice in a warm room?
items.explanation: The correct response is “It can melt.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Heating or cooling can cause physical changes; some are reversible and others are not. Use on-screen observations only.

items.prompt: Which example should be used in the final review?
items.explanation: The correct response is “wax softens when warmed” because it follows the lesson explanation: Heating or cooling can cause physical changes; some are reversible and others are not. Use on-screen observations only.

assetSpecs.prompt: Friendly Grade 3 cartoon storybook concept board for Materials Can Change; rounded shapes, warm dark-brown outlines, tropical palette, no embedded text, uncluttered small-screen layout. Compose overlays provide labels and interactive targets.
assetSpecs.contentDescription: Five connected learning tokens for exploring examples in Materials Can Change.

safetyNotes: Use the on-screen activity only; no unsupervised physical experiment is required.
